quote: My Father is working still, and I am working
firstReading: In Isaiah 49:8-15 the Lord promises restoration and vindication for Israel. God speaks of a time of favor and salvation, lifting up the lowly and restoring justice. The prophet delivers a consoling message: God will gather the dispersed, bring back the exiles, and be attentive to human suffering. The passage emphasizes God’s tender care—He remembers and will not forget His people—turning abandonment into compassion and renewed covenantal fidelity.
psalmSummary: Psalm 145:8-9,13cd-14,17-18 praises the Lord’s compassion, faithfulness, and sovereign care. Mercy, graciousness, and patience characterize God’s reign; the psalmist affirms that the Lord is good to all and watches over those who call upon Him. His kingdom and dominion endure, and He sustains the humble and the oppressed. The faithful are encouraged to trust God’s justice and steadfast love in times of weakness and need.
gospelSummary: In John 5:17-30 Jesus responds to Jewish leaders by explaining His close, authoritative relationship with the Father. He asserts that His work continues because the Father works, claims equality with the Father in judging and giving life, and promises resurrection—both of life and judgment. Jesus says that those who hear His word and believe in the Father who sent Him have eternal life and will be raised on the last day. He also stresses that the Father has entrusted all judgment to the Son, so that honoring the Son is to honor the Father.
saintReflection: Saint Cyril of Jerusalem (Optional Memorial): Saint Cyril of Jerusalem (c. 315-386) was a bishop and Doctor of the Church who demonstrated extraordinary courage defending orthodox Christian faith during the Arian crisis. Appointed bishop around 350 during a period of theological confusion, Cyril steadfastly defended the Nicene Creed's teaching that Jesus Christ was fully divine against the widespread Arian heresy denying Christ's full divinity. His unwavering fidelity cost him dearly: he was exiled three times by emperors sympathetic to Arianism, yet each time he returned to resume his pastoral duties. In 351, a miraculous cross of light surrounded by a rainbow appeared in the sky over Jerusalem—an event witnessed by thousands and which seemed to validate Cyril's theological stance. His greatest contribution came through his Catechetical Lectures delivered in the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, profound instructions on Christian doctrine that remain foundational texts for catechesis. The Council of Constantinople in 381 vindicated his orthodoxy and confirmed his episcopal office. Though persecuted, exiled, and opposed by powerful leaders, Cyril never wavered from proclaiming Christ's full divinity. Declared Doctor of the Church by Pope Leo XIII in 1883, Cyril exemplifies how faithfulness through suffering establishes truth and preserves the apostolic faith for future generations.
dailyPrayer: Lord Jesus, you reveal the Father and share in His life and judgment. Give us the humility to receive your word and the courage to live by it. Where we are tempted to distrust your care, remind us that the Father and you are at work for our salvation. Strengthen leaders and catechists to teach your truth with charity and clarity. Heal divisions in your Church and grant perseverance to those who suffer for faithfulness. May we honor you in word and deed, trusting that you grant life to those who believe. Amen.
theologicalSynthesis: These readings assemble a theological portrait of God as compassionate Redeemer and Jesus as the Father’s uniquely sent Son with authority over life and judgment. Isaiah assures the afflicted of God’s remembrance and restorative action; the Psalm extols divine mercy and faithful rule. In John, Jesus’ claim to work as the Father and to exercise judgment underlines his divine identity and the revelation of the Father through the Son. The tradition, exemplified by Saint Cyril, links doctrinal clarity about Christ’s divinity to pastoral fidelity: right belief safeguards sacramental life and the promise of resurrection. The readings call the faithful to trust God’s mercy and to acknowledge the Son’s mediatory role for salvation and judgment.
exegesis:
Context: John 5 unfolds within a Johannine ministry shaped by conflict with Jewish leaders and deep theological reflection on Jesus’ identity. The preceding narrative (the healing at Bethesda) raised questions about Sabbath observance and authority; Jesus’ subsequent dialogue shifts from a particular controversy to a sustained theological explanation. In this section he reframes healings and works as signs pointing beyond ethical norms to ontological claims: the Son shares the Father’s life-giving activity. The Gospel writer structures Jesus’ speech to move readers from historical incident to cosmic significance, preparing for later revelations about death, resurrection, and divine sending.

Psalm: The selected verses from Psalm 145 emphasize divine compassion, patience, and sovereign care for those who fear the Lord. This liturgical voice links Israel’s trust in God’s steadfast goodness to the hope of vindication and provision. In the lectionary pairing the Psalm functions as a liturgical response that reframes prophetic consolation in personal terms: God’s reign is not abstract but manifests in mercy for the humble and sustained help for those in need. The Psalm’s insistence on praise and remembrance resonates with Isaiah’s assurance that God will not forget His people.

Gospel: John 5:17-30 centers on the relationship between Father and Son. Jesus’ declaration that “My Father is working still, and I am working” situates his ministry within the Father’s ongoing salvific action. He then articulates a set of prerogatives—giving life, raising the dead, and executing judgment—whose possession signals divine authority. Importantly, Jesus grounds his action in hearing and obedience: the Son acts because he hears the Father, and judgment reflects the Word entrusted to him. The Johannine presentation thus ties revelation, obedience, and eschatological destiny together in the person of the Son.

Saints: Turning to Saint Cyril of Jerusalem helps bridge text and tradition. Cyril’s defense of Christ’s full divinity during the Arian crisis shows how ecclesial teaching safeguards biblical interpretation. For Cyril, Christ’s divinity was not a speculative point but central to salvation: if Christ were not truly God, his ability to heal, forgive, and raise would be compromised. Cyril’s catechetical method—clear, pastoral, anchored in Scripture and liturgy—demonstrates how doctrinal clarity undergirds sacramental life and catechesis. His experience of exile and vindication highlights the ecclesial cost and necessity of confessing the Son’s identity.

Today: The passage challenges contemporary hearers to consider the coherence of faith and life: belief in Jesus as Son carries implications for ethics, worship, and hope. The claim that the Father has given all judgment to the Son demands attention to Christ’s mediatory role: honoring the Son is inseparable from honoring the Father. For communities, this translates into catechesis that forms believers to hear the Word, trust God’s mercy, and live in anticipation of resurrection. In contexts of pastoral confusion or doctrinal erosion, Cyril’s witness reminds the Church that charity and doctrinal fidelity must coexist to sustain apostolic faith.

Conclusion: Reading Isaiah’s promise, the Psalm’s praise, and John’s profound portrayal of the Son together yields a theology of compassionate authority: God remembers and restores, rules with mercy, and commissions the Son to reveal, give life, and judge. The Church’s task—teaching, worshiping, and enduring trials—takes shape within that horizon. The lectionary pairing invites a response of trust, fidelity, and renewal: to honor the Son is to honor the Father, to accept mercy is to live hopefully toward resurrection, and to preserve the apostolic confession is to guard the Church’s life for future generations.
